en: Mǎdāo (a freshwater clam/mussel). Taste pungent, slightly cold; toxic. Indicated for metrorrhagia (abnormal uterine bleeding).

Grape. Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for damp painful bi-syndrome of the sinews and bones; boosts qi, redoubles strength, strengthens the will, makes one stout and healthy, and enables one to withstand hunger, wind, and cold. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents aging, and prolongs life. Can be made into wine. Grows in mountain valleys.

Péngléi (wild raspberry). Taste sour, neutral. Mainly calms the five viscera, boosts essence-qi, strengthens and firms yin, strengthens the will and redoubles strength, and aids fertility. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prevents aging. Also called Fùpén. Grows in flat wetlands.

Chinese date (jujube). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for pathogenic qi of the heart and abdomen; calms the middle, nourishes the spleen, assists the twelve channels, calms stomach qi, opens the nine orifices, and supplements deficient qi and fluids, bodily insufficiency, being easily startled, and heaviness of the limbs. Harmonizes the hundred medicinals. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and lengthens life. Its leaves, combined with ephedra, can induce sweating. Grows in flat wetlands.

Lotus seed and rhizome. Taste sweet, neutral. Mainly supplements the middle, nourishes the spirit, boosts qi and strength, and eliminates the hundred diseases. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, resists aging, prevents hunger, and prolongs life. Also called Shuǐzhī ("Water Fungus"). Grows in ponds and marshes.

Qiánshí (foxnut, lit. "chicken head"). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for damp painful bi-syndrome, pain of the lower back, spine, and knees; supplements the middle, eliminates the hundred diseases, boosts essence-qi, strengthens the will, and sharpens hearing and vision. Taken long-term, it lightens the body, prevents hunger, and grants immortal-like longevity. Also called Yànhuì ("wild-goose beak"). Grows in ponds and marshes.
Sweet melon seed. Taste sweet, neutral. Mainly makes one glossy and radiant, gives a good complexion, and boosts qi while preventing hunger. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and resists aging.

Melon pedicle. Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for major water [edema] with swelling of the face and limbs; drains water, kills gu-poison, and treats cough with rising qi. For indigestion after eating fruit, when the ailment lies in the chest and abdomen, it induces vomiting and purging to expel it. Also called Tǔzhī. Grows in flat wetlands.

Winter mallow seed. Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for cold-heat and emaciation of the five viscera and six bowels; breaks the five types of painful urinary dysfunction and disinhibits urination. Taken long-term, it strengthens the bones, builds muscle, lightens the body, and prolongs life.

Amaranth seed. Taste sweet, cold. Indicated for blindness without visible eye damage; brightens the eyes, eliminates pathogenic influences, disinhibits urination and defecation, and clears cold-heat. Taken long-term, it boosts qi and strength, prevents hunger, and lightens the body. Also called Mǎxiàn. Grows in river wetlands.

Bitter lettuce. Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for pathogenic qi of the five viscera, and aversion to food with stomach bi-syndrome. Taken long-term, it calms the heart, boosts qi, sharpens hearing and perception, reduces the need for sleep, lightens the body, and resists aging. Also called Túcǎo, also called Xuǎn. Grows in river valleys.
Cherry. Taste sweet, neutral; non-toxic. Mainly regulates the middle and boosts spleen qi; gives one a good complexion and a pleasant disposition.

Mume fruit (Chinese plum). Taste sour, neutral. Mainly directs qi downward, relieves heat vexation and fullness, calms the heart, and treats pain of the limbs and body, hemiplegia with numbness, and necrotic flesh. Removes dark blemishes and malignant sores. Grows in river valleys.

Smartweed seed. Taste pungent, warm. Mainly brightens the eyes, warms the middle, and enables one to withstand wind and cold; drains water-qi, swelling of the face and eyes, and sores. Mǎliǎo (a smartweed variety) expels leeches from the intestines. Lightens the body. Grows in river wetlands.

Scallion seed. Taste pungent, warm. Mainly brightens the eyes and supplements insufficiency in the middle. Its stalk can be made into a decoction, indicated for cold-heat of cold-damage disorder, to induce sweating, and for wind-stroke with swelling of the face and eyes. Xiè (Chinese onion), taste pungent, indicated for festering wounds from metal injury. [Scallion] lightens the body, prevents hunger, and resists aging. Grows in flat wetlands.

Shuǐsū (Chinese figwort/betony). Taste pungent, slightly warm. Mainly directs qi downward and aids digestion, eliminates bad breath, removes toxicity, and dispels foul qi. Taken long-term, it opens the spirit-intelligence, lightens the body, and resists aging. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Apricot kernel. Taste sweet, warm. Indicated for cough with rising qi, rumbling [in the chest], and throat bi-syndrome; directs qi downward, aids childbirth and lactation, treats metal-wounds, cold in the heart region, and running-piglet qi. Grows in river valleys.

Peach kernel. Taste bitter, neutral. Indicated for stagnant blood, blood blockage, abdominal masses, and pathogenic qi; kills small parasites. Peach blossom expels possessing malevolent spirits and gives one a good complexion. Táoxiāo (a mummified peach left hanging through winter), slightly warm, kills the hundred ghosts and spirit-things. Peach fuzz treats blood masses with abnormal bleeding, cold-heat accumulations, and infertility. Peach-tree borer kills ghosts and inauspicious evil influences. Grows in mountain valleys.
Bitter gourd. Taste bitter, cold. Indicated for major water [edema] with swelling of the face and limbs; drains water. Induces vomiting. Grows in mountain wetlands.

Water celery (water dropwort). Taste sweet, neutral. Indicated for women's reddish vaginal discharge; stops bleeding, nourishes essence, protects the blood vessels, boosts qi, and makes one stout, healthy, and eager to eat. Also called Shuǐyīng. Grows in ponds and marshes.

Sesame. Taste sweet, neutral; non-toxic. Indicated for injury to the middle with deficiency and emaciation; supplements the five internal organs, boosts qi and strength, builds muscle, and fills the brain marrow. Taken long-term, it lightens the body and prevents aging. Its leaf is called Qīngráng; also called Jùshèng. Grows in river valleys.

Hemp flower-fruit clusters. Taste pungent, neutral. Indicated for the seven types of injury; disinhibits the five viscera, and expels stagnant blood and cold qi. Eaten in excess, it makes one see ghosts and run about madly. Taken long-term, it opens the spirit-intelligence and lightens the body. Hemp seed, taste sweet, neutral, mainly supplements the middle and boosts qi. Taken long-term, it makes one stout and healthy and resists aging. Also called Mábó. Grows in river valleys.

Fermented soybean sprout. Taste sweet, neutral; non-toxic. Indicated for damp bi-syndrome with sinew spasm and knee pain. Raw soybean is applied to sores and swelling; boiled and drunk as a decoction, it kills gu-poison and stops pain. Red adzuki bean drains water and expels pus and blood from sores and swellings. Grows in flat wetlands.

Foxtail millet. Taste bitter; non-toxic. Mainly nourishes kidney qi, removes heat from the stomach and spleen, and boosts qi. The aged grain, bitter in taste, treats stomach heat, wasting-thirst disorder, and disinhibits urination.
Glutinous millet. Taste sweet; non-toxic. Mainly boosts qi and supplements the middle; in excess it is heating and can cause vexation.
Fǔbì. Taste pungent, neutral. Indicated for malarial disorders with cold-heat, pathogenic qi, and diarrhea; treats impotence and headache from wine-drinking.
